coredata.dc:title: A metal ion-drug-induced self-assembly nanosystems for augmented chemodynamic and chemotherapy synergetic anticancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dong-Hui
Li, Chao-Qing
Hou, Xiao-Lin
Wu, Gui-Ying
Xie, Xiao-Ting
Zhu, Dan
Jin, Fang
Zhao, Yuan-Di
Liu, Bo
coredata.dc:description: The complexities in the integration of carrier materials and functional materials make it challenging to promote nanoprobes for clinical translation. Carrier-free self-assembled nanosystems have been proposed as a promising strategy for synergetic anticancer therapy. In this study, carbon dots, copper ions, and doxorubicin (DOX) were assembled into nanoparticles (CCD) to achieve augmented chemodynamic therapy (CDT). The assembled CCD NPs were biodegradable and responsive to GSH and acidic pH in the tumor microenvironment resulting in the release of the DOX, Cu2+, and carbon dots. The intracellular H2O2 level was elevated by DOX activated the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate oxidases. The GSH was depleted by Cu2+, and the generated Cu+ as well as peroxidase-like carbon dots could catalyze the intracellular H2O2 to produce cytotoxic ·OH to achieve enhanced CDT effects. Chemotherapy effects were enhanced through increasing drug sensitivity and inhibiting drug efflux after the intracellular redox balance was broken by CCD NPs. The in vivo experiments revealed that CCD NPs possessed the excellent biocompatibility and synergistic anti-tumor ability, which could completely inhibit the growth of 4T1 tumors. As a novel carrier-free nanoprobes, CCD NPs with responsiveness to the tumor microenvironment may have great potential in cancer chemodynamic therapy with high specificity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carrier-free
Self-assembly
Chemodynamic therapy
Peroxidase-like
Synergetic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S-doped carbon dots
Peroxidase activity assay of carbon dots and CCD NPs
The release of DOX and copper ions
Antitumor effect of CCD in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFE0113400), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81871414, 81971658, 91959109), and the Opening Project of Hubei key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology (40201005). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2018YFE0113400 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81871414 , 81971658 , 91959109 ), and the Opening Project of Hubei key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology ( 40201005 ). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthetic process of the CCD NPs and the CCD NPs mediated chemotherapy and chemodynamic synergistic therapy of tumors. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of CCD NPs. TEM image of carbon dots (CDs) (A) and CCD NPs (B), inset is magnifications of the CCD NP. TEM image of CCD NPs after incubated with GSH (C). UV–vis absorption spectra (D), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra (E), and FT-IR spectra (F) of the CCD NPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The mechanism of the CCD NPs assembly. TEM images of the Cu2++carbon dots (A), carbon dots + DOX (B), Cu2++DOX (C), and Cu2++DOX + carbon dots without PVP (D).

The assay of GSH depletion and ·OH generation of CCD NPs. The peroxidase activity assay of carbon dots (A) and CCD NPs (B). The GSH depletion of the CCD NPs with different times (C) and different concentrations (D). The ·OH generation of the CCD NPs using MB with different times (E) and different concentrations (F). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

GSH responsiveness of CCD NPs. The XPS spectra of Cu in CCD NPs and CCD NPs reacting with GSH (A). The ·OH generation of CCD NPs detected by DMPO in presence of GSH and H2O2 (B). The release of DOX (C) and copper ions (D) in the presence of GSH and acidic conditions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The biocompatibility of CCD NPs in vivo. Representative images of the H&E-stained major organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and small intestine (A), and the routine blood test and blood biochemical analysis, including alanine aminotransferase (ALT) (B), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) (C), hemoglobin (HGB) (D), platelet (PLT) (E), white blood cell (WBC) (F), and red blood cell (RBC) (G) after three intravenous injections of PBS and the CCD NPs (n = 5) for 30 d. Scale bars: 50 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The intracellular ·OH generation and anticancer ability of CCD NPs against 4T1 cells. The intracellular ·OH generation of cells incubated with CDs, CDs + Cu2+, DOX, and CCD NPs (A). The viability test of cells using fluorescence (B) and CCK-8 (C), and the intracellular GSH concentration assay (D) after incubated with CDs, CDs + Cu2+, DOX, and CCD NPs. (Scar bars are 20 μm, ∗∗∗P < 0.001).

The antitumor ability of CCD NPs in vivo. Photographs of representative mice (A) and tumors (B) excised in mice given different treatments. Tumor growth curves (C) and body weight changes (D) of mice that received different treatments. TUNEL assay (E) and H&E analysis (F) of tumor tissue from different groups. Data are exhibited as the mean ± SD (n = 4). Scale bars are 100 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: A metal ion-drug-induced self-assembly nanosystems for augmented chemodynamic and chemotherapy synergetic anticancer therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complexities in the integration of carrier materials and functional materials make it challenging to promote nanoprobes for clinical translation. Carrier-free self-assembled nanosystems have been proposed as a promising strategy for synergetic anticancer therapy. In this study, carbon dots, copper ions, and doxorubicin (DOX) were assembled into nanoparticles (CCD) to achieve augmented chemodynamic therapy (CDT). The assembled CCD NPs were biodegradable and responsive to GSH and acidic pH in the tumor microenvironment resulting in the release of the DOX, Cu2+, and carbon dots. The intracellular H2O2 level was elevated by DOX activated the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate oxidases. The GSH was depleted by Cu2+, and the generated Cu+ as well as peroxidase-like carbon dots could catalyze the intracellular H2O2 to produce cytotoxic ·OH to achieve enhanced CDT effects. Chemotherapy effects were enhanced through increasing drug sensitivity and inhibiting drug efflux after the intracellular redox balance was broken by CCD NPs. The in vivo experiments revealed that CCD NPs possessed the excellent biocompatibility and synergistic anti-tumor ability, which could completely inhibit the growth of 4T1 tumors. As a novel carrier-free nanoprobes, CCD NPs with responsiveness to the tumor microenvironment may have great potential in cancer chemodynamic therapy with high specificity.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy remains the main strategy of cancer treatment besides surgery and radiotherapy. Chemotherapy is able to eliminate residual lesions that failed to remove by radiotherapy and surgery, prolonging the survival of patients [1]. The chemotherapy drugs DOX and cisplatin are widely used in clinical, but their application is limited by severe side effects and multidrug resistance. Therefore, improving the efficiency of drug delivery and overcoming multidrug resistance are essential for chemotherapy [2]. The developments of nanotechnology hold new promises for drug delivery and enhanced chemotherapy efficacy. For example, nanomedicine can achieve targeted drug delivery, reducing drug damage to normal tissues and increasing drug accumulation at tumor sites [3]. In addition, combining chemotherapy with photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) can enhance the effects of chemotherapy [4,5]. The glutathione (GSH) depletion and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation at the tumor microenvironment by nanomaterials can break the redox balance of the tumor microenvironment to overcome multidrug resistance [6,7]. Therefore, the combination of chemotherapy drug and nanomaterials can enhance the effect of chemotherapy through synergistic treatment.

CDT kills cancer cells by cytotoxic ·OH initiated by metal ions reaction with endogenous overexpressed H2O2, which is not limited by the penetration depth of light and can kill deep-seated tumors with high specificity [8,9]. However, CDT development has several urgent issues to be addressed. The Fe2+ catalyzed Fenton reaction has a low reaction rate (∼63 M−1 s−1) and requires acidic conditions [10]. The GSH in the tumor microenvironment can scavenge generated ·OH, making CDT treatment less efficient [11]. Although the H2O2 content of the tumor microenvironment is higher than that in normal tissues (10–100 μM) [12], sustained tumor killing is difficult to achieve. Compared with Fe2+, Cu+ has a higher reaction rate (1 × 104 M−1 s−1, 160-fold over that of Fe2+) with H2O2 and is not governed by pH environment [13]. In addition, chemotherapy drugs, such as DOX and cisplatin, can promote the generation of H2O2 through activating the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) oxidases (NOXs) [14,15]. Therefore, combining copper ion-mediated CDT and DOX-mediated chemotherapy can improve the efficiency of tumor treatment.

Nanocarriers suffer from several limitations in delivery anticancer drugs by encapsulation or covalent conjugation, including time-consuming and laborious to integrate carrier materials with functional materials, complex chemical modifications, and low drug loading capacity [16,17]. To address these issues, carrier-free nanoprobes have been developed. Carrier-free nanoprobes are prepared by self-assembly of anticancer drugs in aqueous solution, driven by some intermolecular forces, such as hydrophobic interactions, π-π stacking, and metal ion coordination interactions [18,19]. For example, DOX can be assembled with carbon nanomaterials through hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking [20,21]; metal ions have high affinity to DOX and carbon dots and can be assembled through coordination interactions [22–25]. Thus, copper ions, DOX, and carbon dots are expected to form a new carrier-free drug delivery for tumor therapy.

In this work, we developed carrier-free nanoprobes for tumor chemodynamic and chemotherapy synergistic treatment by a one-step rapid assembly of copper ions, carbon dots, and DOX (abbreviated as CCD, C: copper ions; C: carbon dots; D: DOX). In tumor microenvironment, the fast biodegradation of the CCD NPs by GSH complex with Cu2+ and pH breaking the coordination interaction resulted in the release of the Cu2+, carbon dots, and DOX. Enhanced tumor chemodynamic therapy was achieved through GSH depletion, increased H2O2 levels, and high catalytic efficiency by peroxidase activity of carbon dots and Fenton-like reactions of copper ion. At the same time, the chemotherapy effect was enhanced by GSH depletion and ROS generation to disrupt the intracellular redox balance (Fig. 1 ). In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that the CCD NPs had the ability of synergistic CDT and chemotherapy to achieve complete suppression of tumor growth. This approach provides a new strategy for the design and synthesis of carrier-free nanoprobes for synergistic therapy of tumors.

DOX and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and calcine-AM were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. H2O2, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), methylene blue (MB), citric acid, thiourea, and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), reduced glutathione(GSH), 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid (DTNB), and Cu(NO3)2 were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China).

The size and morphology of CCD NPs were observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (HT7700, HITACHI, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were performed on a VERTEX 70 (Bruker, Germany). Hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential were measured by a Nano-ZS90 Zeta sizer (Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S-doped carbon dots were synthesized according to the slightly modified method in the literature [26]. In brief, 2 g citric acid and 4 g thiourea were added to 20 mL DMF, and the mixture was sonicated to dissolve completely. The mixed solution was transferred into a 15 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water to remove residual reagents.

Carbon dots-Cu2+ nanoassembly was obtained by the assembly of carbon dots and Cu2+. 2 mL of carbon dots were added in 2 mL of PBS, and the mixture were stirred at room temperature for 5 min. 2 mL Cu2+ (10 mM) was added dropwise to the mixed solution and stirred for 12 h. Carbon dots-Cu2+ nanoassembly was obtained by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min.

PBS (2 mL) and carbon dots (3 mL) were mixed with 10 μL DOX (1 mg mL−1), and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 5 min. 2 mL Cu2+ (10 mM) was added dropwise to the mixed solution and stirred for 30 min. 10 mg PVP was added in the mixture, and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min. The CCD NPs were obtained by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase activity assay of carbon dots and CCD NPs
ce:section.ce:para: 50 mL TMB (16 mM) and 200 μL H2O2 (5 mM) were mixed with 750 μL carbon dots or CCD NPs, respectively. TMB + H2O2, TMB + carbon dots/CCD NPs, H2O2+carbon dots/CCD NPs were used as controls. UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixed solutions at 650 nm were measured after 30 min.

1 mL CCD NPs (1.8 mg mL−1) were mixed with 30 μL GSH (100 mM) and 970 μL H2O for different time. After the solution was centrifuged, the supernatants were incubated with 12 μL 10 mg mL−1 DTNB, and the absorbance at 412 nm was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The release of DOX and copper ions
ce:section.ce:para: CCD NPs (1 mL, 10 mg mL−1) was loaded into dialysis bag and placed in the buffer (15 mL, pH 5 or 7) with or without GSH (10 mM). Dialysis solution (3 mL) was taken out and 3 mL fresh buffer was added at different time. The absorption of the solution was tested at different time to calculate the concentration of DOX. The concentration of copper ions was measured by an atomic absorption spectrometer.
CCD NPs (1 mL, 1.8 mg mL−1) was incubated with 300 μL 100 mM GSH, 30 μL 100 mg L−1 MB, 30 μL 100 mM H2O2, 1640 μL H2O for different times. The absorbance at 650 nm was measured.

A multiple-dosage toxicity test was conducted to assess the biocompatibility of the CCD NPs. Ten female BALB/c mice (6 weeks) were randomly divided into two groups (n = 5) and injected intravenously with 100 μL CCD NPs (1.8 mg mL−1) or PBS every 3 days for three times. After 30 days, the mice were sacrificed, and the major organs were harvested for histological analysis. After the tissues were embedded in paraffin and sectioned, the slices were stained using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and observed under an Olympus BX53 microscope. The blood was collected for blood biochemical analysis and routine blood test.

4T1 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and cultured overnight. The cells were incubated with carbon dots, the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+, DOX, or CCD NPs (600 μg mL−1) for 4 h. The intracellular ROS was detected by ROS fluorescence probe DCFH-DA according to the manufacturer's instruction.

4T1 cells were incubated with carbon dots, the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+, DOX, or CCD NPs (600 μg mL−1) for 6 h. After cells were collected, broken, and centrifuged, the supernatant was obtained to detect the intracellular GSH content using GSH assay kit (Solarbio, China) according to the manufacturer's instruction.

4T1 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and cultured overnight. The cells were incubated with carbon dots, the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+, DOX, or CCD NPs (600 μg mL−1) for 4 h. The ratio of living cells to dead cells was determined after cells were stained by calcein-AM and PI. The viability of cells was measured quantitatively using CCK-8 after cells with different treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antitumor effect of CCD in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model, female BALB/c mice (6 weeks) were injected subcutaneously with 100 μL 106 cells/mL 4T1 cells. When the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, twenty 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into five groups (n = 4). The mice from each group were injected intratumorally with 50 μL PBS, carbon dots, the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+, DOX, and CCD NPs (5 mg mL−1), respectively. The volume of the tumor and the body weight of the mice were measured every two days. To further assess the antitumor effects, the tumor tissue from different groups were harvested for H&E and TUNEL staining.

CCD NPs were formed by self-assembly of copper ions, carbon dots, and DOX (Fig. S1). The carbon dots were synthesized from citric acid and thiourea by high temperature hydrothermal reaction, and were further used after dialysis (Fig. S2). The size of carbon dots was less than 5 nm (Fig. 2 A). The spherical CCD nanoparticles with an average size of 76.89 nm were formed after assembly (Fig. 2B), showing good dispersion and uniform size. The high magnification TEM image showed that carbon dots were employed as build blocks uniformly embedded in each CCD NPs (Fig. 2B). Since GSH-Cu2+ complexation could break the coordination interaction [27], and acid pH could weaken the electrostatic interaction between carbon dots, Cu2+, and DOX [28], CCD NPs were disassembled to release them after incubated with GSH (Fig. 2C), and the hydrodynamic size of CCD NPs changed from 91.28 nm to 18.17 nm (Fig. S3), demonstrating that CCD NPs were GSH/pH-responsive and biodegradable. To further investigate the successful synthesis of CCD NPs, we performed UV–vis absorption spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra, and FT-IR spectra. UV–vis absorption spectra illustrated that the CCD NPs were assembled from carbon dots, copper ions, and DOX. The quality ratio of copper in CCD NPs was 5.7% measured by an atomic absorption spectrometer. The absorption peak of the CCD NPs at 337 nm and 480 nm was attributed to the characteristic absorption peak of carbon dots and DOX, respectively (Fig. 2D). The NIR absorption peak at 700–900 nm was attributed to the presence of copper ions [29]. XPS spectra of the carbon dots demonstrated the presence of N and S in the carbon dots (Fig. S4), and the peaks of S 2p, N 1s and Cu 2p in the XPS spectra of the CCD NPs demonstrated the presence of carbon dots and copper ions (Fig. 2E, Fig. S5). The resemblance of FT-IR spectra between the pure DOX and CCD NPs about the bands at 1278 cm−1 (O–C–C stretching), 993 cm−1 (C–O stretching of alcohol), and 688-865 cm−1 (out of plane O–H bending) confirmed the presence of DOX in the CCD NPs (Fig. 2F) [30].

The mechanism of CCD NPs assembly was investigated. The zeta potential of carbon dots was −30.3 mV (Fig. S6), which was attributed to the abundant carboxyl groups. The carbon dots have a great affinity with copper ions and could assemble with copper ions to form star-like nanostructures through electrostatic interactions and coordination interactions (Fig. 3 A). Carbon dots could also assemble with DOX to form spherical nanoparticles through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions (Fig. 3B), which served as a carrier to delivery DOX for cancer therapy. Copper ions could assemble with DOX to form irregular nanostructures through coordination interactions (Fig. 3C). The assembly of carbon dots, DOX, and copper ions without PVP could form spherical nanoparticles with larger size (Fig. 3D), which was prone to aggregate due to the absence of stabilization and size limitation by PVP. The good stability of the CCD NPs was obtained by PVP encapsulation (Fig. S7), and the zeta potential of CCD NPs was −11.06 mV (Fig. S8). These results indicated that CCD NPs were assembled by pairwise interactions of carbon dots, DOX, and copper ions and stabilized by PVP.

Previously reported studies showed that the N and S-doped carbon dots presented nanozyme activity [31,32]. TMB was used to detect the enzymatic activity of carbon dots and CCD NPs. Compared with the control, the carbon dots mixed with TMB and H2O2 had a significant absorption peak at 650 nm (Fig. 4 A), which was due to the presence of ·OH in the solution through peroxidase-like carbon dots catalyzing H2O2 (Fig. S9). Because the copper ions can increase the enzymatic activity of carbon dots [33,34], significantly enhanced absorption peaks at 650 nm was observed after CCD NPs mixed with TMB and H2O2 (Fig. 4B), which indicated enhanced catalytic efficiency of CCD NPs than carbon dots.

Glutathione depletion ability of CCD NPs was also detected. The Cu2+ and GSH undergo a redox reaction to generate Cu+ and GSSG leading to GSH depleting. DTNB was used to monitor the process of GSH depletion. The colorless DTNB could be reduced by GSH to form the yellow products, and its absorption peak at 412 nm was monitored. Compared with the control (Fig. S10), the absorption at 412 nm gradually decreased to 4.7% after CCD NPs (600 μg mL−1) mixed with GSH for 6 h (Fig. 4C), indicating that the GSH concentration in the solution gradually decreased. Meanwhile, the absorption at 412 nm gradually decreased with the increase of CCD NPs concentration after GSH mixed with different concentration of CCD NPs (Fig. 4D), suggesting that the ability of CCD NPs to consume GSH was concentration-dependent. These results indicated that the CCD NPs possessed great performance to deplete GSH.

The generated Cu+ from the reaction of Cu2+ with GSH can catalyze H2O2 via Fenton-like reaction to generate ·OH. The ability of CCD NPs to generate ·OH was detected by MB, which can be degraded by the generated ·OH. The absorption of MB at 650 nm gradually decreased to 37.3% after CCD NPs (150 μg mL−1) was mixed with GSH, H2O2, and MB for 12 h (Fig. 4E), indicating that ·OH was sustainably produced. The absorption of MB decreased from 66.8% to 9.8% with the concentration of CCD NPs increasing from 50 to 600 μg mL−1 suggesting that the ability of CCD NPs to produce ·OH was concentration-dependent (Fig. 4F). These results indicated that CCD NPs could catalyze H2O2 to generate ·OH. Therefore, CCD NPs is expected as a nanoprobe for the chemodynamic therapy of tumors.

The responsiveness of CCD NPs to GSH was further investigated. The binding energy of Cu changed from 952.56 eV to 932.76 eV–952.49 eV and 932.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 5 A), and the disappearance of the peak at 944.12 eV confirmed that the valence state of copper changed from +2 to +1 after reduced by GSH [10]. After reacted with GSH, the generated Cu + could convert H2O2 to ·OH (Fig. 5B). The release of DOX and copper ions was accelerated in the presence of GSH and acidic condition (Fig. 5C and D), demonstrating the responsiveness of CCD NPs to the tumor microenvironment.

The biocompatibility and safety were evaluated by injected intravenously with CCD NPs three times. The major organs and blood of the mice were collected after 30 days. The major organs including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and small intestine underwent paraffin embedding, slicing and H&E staining, and no significant physiopathology change was observed in the organs treated with CCD NPs (Fig. 6 A). Routine blood test and blood biochemical analysis were further performed including alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), platelets (PLT), and hemoglobin (HGB), and these parameters from both CCD NPs treated group and the control group were within the normal range (Fig. 6B). These results indicated that the CCD NPs had good biocompatibility and safety to apply in cancer therapy in vivo.

The intracellular ROS was detected using DCFH-DA, which can be transformed to non-fluorescent DCFH through intracellular esterase deacetylation and further be oxidized by ·OH to emit a green fluorescence. The negligible fluorescence in the PBS group was due to the intrinsic ROS of cells which were endogenic byproducts of cell metabolism and indispensable in the process of intracellular signal transduction (Fig. 7 A) [35]. Cells incubated with carbon dots showed weak green fluorescence demonstrating a little ·OH production through catalyzing intracellular H2O2 by the peroxidase-like carbon dots. The cells incubated with the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+ exhibited an enhanced fluorescent signal due to the GSH depletion and the increased catalytic efficiency by copper ions. However, the production of ·OH was limited due to insufficient H2O2. Cells incubated with DOX also have a weak green fluorescence which may be due to O2 ·̶ generation through DOX activating the NADPH oxidases [36]. In contrast, a strong green fluorescence can be observed in the cells incubated with the CCD NPs, indicating a large amount of ·OH production. This result was ascribed to the GSH consumption, the increased catalytic efficiency, as well as improved intracellular H2O2 levels.

The antitumor effect of CCD NPs was determined by cell live/dead staining (Fig. 7B). Cells in the control group showed strong green fluorescence, indicating good growth condition of the cells. The decreased green fluorescence and increased red fluorescence were observed in the cells incubated with carbon dots and the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+, indicating that the cell growth was inhibited by the production of ·OH. The intensity of green fluorescence and red fluorescence were comparable for cells incubated with DOX, indicating that chemotherapy did not completely inhibit the growth of the cells. Strong red fluorescence was observed in cells incubated with the CCD NPs, indicating that cell activity was low and cell growth was well inhibited. The viability of cells suffering different treatments was measured quantitatively using CCK-8 (Fig. 7C), suggesting that CCD NPs had the best anti-cancer performance. The intracellular GSH concentrations were calculated after the cells were under different treatments (Fig. 7D). The results showed that the good anticancer performance of CCD NPs was driven by the tremendous depletion of the intracellular GSH. These results suggested that the CCD NPs has an enhanced chemodynamic therapy effect against 4T1 cells.

4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 5 groups to investigate the antitumor effects of the CCD NPs in vivo by monitoring the tumor volume of the mice from each group. Compared with the PBS group, the tumor volume of carbon dots-treated mice was slightly reduced (Fig. 8 A), indicating a slight antitumor effect of peroxidase activity of carbon dots. The tumor growth rate of the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+ or DOX-treated mice was slow but not completely inhibited due to the single chemodynamic therapy by the complex of carbon dots and Cu2+ and chemotherapy by DOX (Fig. 8C). In contrast, due to the synergistic treatment of chemotherapy and CDT, the tumors of mice treated with the CCD NPs were completely eliminated after 18 days of treatment (Fig. 8B), indicating the significant antitumor effect of the CCD NPs. There was no significant change in the body weight of the mice during different treatments (Fig. 8D), further demonstrating that the CCD NPs presented a long-term biocompatibility. The intense immunofluorescence of TUNEL showed more tumor cell apoptosis in the groups of CCD NPs (Fig. 8E). H&E staining results suggested that cells without intact nuclei were observed in the tumor tissues treated with CCD NPs (Fig. 8F).

In this study, we integrated carbon dots, copper ions, and DOX in one nanoparticle by a simple and rapid method, which were self-assembled through hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic interactions, and coordination interactions. The CCD NPs were degraded after GSH complex with Cu2+ and acidic pH breaking the coordination interaction leading to the release of the Cu2+, carbon dots, and DOX. The in vitro experiments confirmed that the CCD NPs had the ability to catalyze intracellular H2O2 to produce ·OH via peroxidase-like carbon dots and Fenton-like reaction of copper ions. Combined with GSH depletion and DOX promoted H2O2 production, the CCD NPs could enhance chemodynamic therapy. The in vivo experiments revealed that the CCD NPs had good biocompatibility and excellent performance to completely inhibit the tumor growth by synergistic chemotherapy and chemodynamic therapy. This work provides a promising insight for exploring the construction of tumor microenvironment-responsive carrier-free nanosystems by self-assembly to improve synergistic antitumor effect.

Dong-Hui Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Chao-Qing Li: Visualization, Formal analysis. Xiao-Lin Hou: Methodology. Gui-Ying Wu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Xiao-Ting Xie: Formal analysis. Dan Zhu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Fang Jin: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yuan-Di Zhao: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Bo Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFE0113400), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81871414, 81971658, 91959109), and the Opening Project of Hubei key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology (40201005). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.
